comparison_checkpoints.details: Confirm the customer host, Rocky 8 baseline, and build markers line up with the trusted lab appliance before deeper diagnosis.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare appliance identity first

comparison_checkpoints.details: Verify that the critical services below appear active in the customer environment and match the expected enabled state.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare critical service state

comparison_checkpoints.details: Use Apache proxy config and local port mappings to confirm the expected AlgoSec routes still point at the right local services.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare routed product endpoints

comparison_checkpoints.details: Use the listening-port count and service listener hints as a quick baseline for what should be reachable or bound on the appliance.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare visible listeners

decision_playbooks.decision_rule: If a step fails, stop the happy-path sequence, record that step as the current failure point, and collect the matching evidence before moving on.

dependency_path.details: Start here. Check storage, inode, memory, OOM, load, and CPU pressure to decide whether the Linux host can still support Apache, Keycloak, Metro, logging, and temp-file writes.
dependency_path.label: Host can support useful work

dependency_path.details: If the host looks healthy, confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route requests into the auth and app branches.
dependency_path.label: Apache/HTTPD serving the UI

dependency_path.details: Check whether the legacy auth-trigger hop reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then confirm the later Keycloak and FireFlow handoff without pretending they are the first observed neighbor.
dependency_path.label: Auth handoff can do useful work

dependency_path.details: Check whether ms-metro is serving useful app traffic and showing healthy JVM behavior, not only whether port 8080 is open.
dependency_path.label: App branch can do useful work

dependency_path.details: If the host and both branches still look up, use Apache/HTTPD, Keycloak, and Metro clues to find the first clear stop point.
dependency_path.label: Useful work stops here

decision_playbooks.label: ASMS UI is down

steps.action: Check whether the host can support useful work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm storage, inode, memory, load, OOM, and CPU pressure are not already blocking Apache, Keycloak, Metro, log writing, or temp-file work.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat disk, inode, memory, load, OOM, or CPU pressure on the host as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Host pressure signals from df -h, df -ih, free -h, and uptime.
Any recent OOM lines and the process currently owning CPU pressure.
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat disk, inode, memory, load, OOM, or CPU pressure on the host as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If the host looks healthy, move to the Apache edge checks.
steps.next_if_pass: If the host looks healthy, move to the Apache edge checks.

recommended_commands.example_output:
Filesystem           Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/rl-root   60G   16G   45G  27% /
/dev/mapper/rl-data  238G   21G  218G   9% /data
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Runtime filesystems still have enough free space for logs, temp files, and service work.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If /, /boot, or /data is close to full, Apache, Keycloak, Metro, installers, and log writers can all fail or behave unpredictably.
recommended_commands.label: Check storage pressure on runtime filesystems

recommended_commands.example_output:
Filesystem          Inodes IUsed IFree IUse% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/rl-root    30M  343K   30M    2% /
/dev/mapper/rl-data   119M   21K  119M    1% /data
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Runtime filesystems still have free inodes for logs, temp files, sockets, and service output.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If inode use is high, the host can look like it has free disk while Apache, Keycloak, Metro, or log rotation still fail to create files.
recommended_commands.label: Check inode pressure on runtime filesystems

recommended_commands.example_output:
total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           32Gi        13Gi       8.6Gi       2.4Gi         9Gi        15Gi
Swap:          24Gi          0B        24Gi
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Available memory is still present and swap is not carrying active pressure for the host.
healthy_markers: Mem and Swap are shown in GiB
recommended_commands.interpretation: If available memory is very low or swap is heavily used, Java services like Keycloak and Metro may slow down, hang, fail health checks, or get killed later.
recommended_commands.label: Check memory pressure on JVM-backed services

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Load is not unexpectedly high for the host and does not already explain the UI symptom.
healthy_markers: load values are not unexpectedly high
recommended_commands.interpretation: If load is unusually high, the system may be under CPU pressure, blocked work, or heavy I/O wait before you even reach the UI-specific branches.
recommended_commands.label: Check current host load pressure

recommended_commands.command: journalctl -k --since "24 hours ago" --no-pager | grep -i -E "out of memory|oom|killed process"
recommended_commands.example_output: No output
recommended_commands.expected_signal: No recent Out Of Memory lines are returned.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If OOM lines appear, the host has already been killing or starving processes under memory pressure, so downstream UI symptoms may only be the visible side effect.
recommended_commands.label: Check recent memory-kill pressure

recommended_commands.example_output:
PID COMMAND         %CPU %MEM
 6012 java             8.1  7.4
 1018 httpd            1.2  0.4
 3758 algosec_keycloa  0.8  1.6
recommended_commands.expected_signal: No unexpected process is consuming enough CPU to starve the rest of the ASMS path.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If one process is consuming most of the CPU, treat that process as part of the current system pressure story before assuming the UI path itself is the root cause.
recommended_commands.label: Check which process owns CPU pressure right now

steps.why_this_matters: Every major playbook starts with the Linux host. The first question is not whether a number looks high. It is whether current host pressure is already enough to break useful work in the UI system.

steps.action: Check whether Apache/HTTPD can do useful edge work for the ASMS UI path. Confirm httpd.service is running, 80 and 443 are listening, the suite login route still lands on the Apache-served UI page, representative /algosec-ui/ assets still load through Apache, and the proxy mapping still points toward the auth and app branches.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat the Apache edge, local response path, or proxy mapping as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Apache service and listener state from systemctl status and ss -lntp.
The suite login redirect, the Apache-served UI login page, representative /algosec-ui/ assets, and a proxy route snippet that show where UI traffic goes next.
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat the Apache edge, local response path, or proxy mapping as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If Apache still serves the login entry page, the representative UI assets, and the edge still points toward the auth and app branches, move to the first auth-trigger checks.
steps.next_if_pass: If Apache still serves the login entry page, the representative UI assets, and the edge still points toward the auth and app branches, move to the first auth-trigger checks.

recommended_commands.example_output:
● httpd.service - The Apache HTTP Server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/httpd.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:37:12 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 1018 (/usr/sbin/httpd)
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If this is not active, treat the edge service itself as the failure point before going deeper.
recommended_commands.label: Check httpd.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: A listening socket is present for 80, 443.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If 80/443 are missing, the UI path is failing at the listener or bind layer.
recommended_commands.label: Check edge listeners

recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Server: Apache
Location: https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Apache returns a redirect from the legacy suite login path into the current UI login page.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the suite login route stops redirecting here, the edge path is already broken before Keycloak or Metro have a chance to help.
recommended_commands.label: Check the legacy suite login route

recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: Apache
Content-Type: text/html; charset=UTF-8
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Apache returns the current UI login page with HTTP 200.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If Apache cannot serve the login page here, stop at the edge before chasing the auth or app neighbors.
recommended_commands.label: Check the Apache-served UI login page

recommended_commands.command: for path in /algosec-ui/styles.css /algosec-ui/runtime.js /algosec-ui/main.js; do echo "=== $path ==="; curl -k -sS -D - -o /tmp/asms-ui-asset.out "https://127.0.0.1$path" | sed -n '1,8p'; printf 'BODY_BYTES '; wc -c < /tmp/asms-ui-asset.out; done
recommended_commands.example_output:
=== /algosec-ui/styles.css ===
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: Apache
Content-Type: text/css
BODY_BYTES 758180
=== /algosec-ui/runtime.js ===
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Content-Type: application/javascript
BODY_BYTES 2737
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Apache returns HTTP 200 for representative CSS and JS assets and each body is non-empty.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the login HTML works but these assets fail, return the wrong type, or come back empty, the edge is not yet browser-useful even before Keycloak or Metro are checked.
recommended_commands.label: Check representative /algosec-ui/ assets through Apache

recommended_commands.example_output:
78:RewriteRule "^/algosec/suite/login(.*)$" "/algosec-ui/login" [R]
299:AliasMatch (?i)^/algosec-ui/(.*)$ /usr/share/fa/suite/client/app/suite-new-ui/$1
1:<Location /keycloak/>
2:        ProxyPass https://localhost:8443/ timeout=300
139:<Location /afa/api/v1>
140:  ProxyPass http://localhost:8080/afa/api/v1 timeout=18000 retry=0
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Apache config still shows the login redirect, the UI alias, and the auth and app route mapping needed by the ASMS UI path.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the expected proxy targets are missing or wrong, the edge can look healthy while routing useful work to the wrong place or nowhere useful.
recommended_commands.label: Check Apache routes for login, auth, and app branches

steps.why_this_matters: After host health, Apache/HTTPD is the system edge for the UI path. The real question is whether the edge can still serve browser-useful login content and hand auth and app requests to the right neighbors, not only whether a process exists.

steps.action: Check the first auth-triggering chain after the static shell. On this lab, the browser first touches legacy suite setup and session-validation paths, then reaches BusinessFlow as the first named operational checkpoint, then proxied Keycloak token paths and FireFlow auth checks before the final handoff into `/afa/php/home.php`.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat the first auth-triggering hop you actually reproduced as the current failure point. If the reproduced journey still never leaves the static shell, say the first auth neighbor remains unproven. If it reaches the legacy setup hop but never reaches BusinessFlow, stay on the earlier auth-trigger path. If it reaches BusinessFlow but not the later Keycloak or FireFlow handoff, keep the failure point on the BusinessFlow-to-auth transition instead of skipping ahead.
evidence_to_collect: Auth and app branch evidence from systemctl status, listener output, and local useful-work checks for Keycloak and Metro.
Recent Apache, Keycloak, and Metro clues only after the host and edge checks narrow the likely stop point.
steps.failure_point: the first auth-triggering hop after the static shell, the BusinessFlow checkpoint behind it, or the later Keycloak and FireFlow-backed auth handoff
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat the first auth-triggering hop you actually reproduced as the current failure point. If the reproduced journey still never leaves the static shell, say the first auth neighbor remains unproven. If it reaches the legacy setup hop but never reaches BusinessFlow, stay on the earlier auth-trigger path. If it reaches BusinessFlow but not the later Keycloak or FireFlow handoff, keep the failure point on the BusinessFlow-to-auth transition instead of skipping ahead.
steps.if_pass: If the first auth-triggering hop, the BusinessFlow checkpoint, and the later Keycloak and FireFlow handoff all look healthy, move to the app branch checks.
steps.next_if_pass: If the first auth-triggering hop, the BusinessFlow checkpoint, and the later Keycloak and FireFlow handoff all look healthy, move to the app branch checks.

recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 200
Server: Apache-Coyote
{  "isSSOEnabled" : false,
  "needsFirstTimeSetup" : false,
  "isAffEnabled" : true,
recommended_commands.expected_signal: The setup endpoint returns JSON that shows the current login mode and whether SSO is enabled on this appliance.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If this endpoint fails, the first auth-trigger path is already broken before credentials are submitted. If it shows `"isSSOEnabled" : false`, do not assume Keycloak is the first real auth hop for this journey.
recommended_commands.label: Check the shipped login setup endpoint first

recommended_commands.command: rm -f /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies /tmp/asms-auth-hop.headers /tmp/asms-auth-hop.body; curl -k -sS -L -c /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies -b /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies -A 'Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/123.0.0.0 Safari/537.36' -H 'Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8' -H 'Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9' -H 'Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1' -D /tmp/asms-auth-hop.headers -o /tmp/asms-auth-hop.body https://127.0.0.1/afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php; sed -n '1,20p' /tmp/asms-auth-hop.headers; echo '--- cookies ---'; cat /tmp/asms-auth-hop.cookies 2>/dev/null
recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Set-Cookie: PHPSESSID=pp3eujujutk0t1mrmvfd6jflrm; path=/; secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax
Location: /algosec-ui/login?last_url=%2Fafa%2Fphp%2FSuiteLoginSessionValidation.php
recommended_commands.expected_signal: The legacy session-validation path either redirects back to `/algosec-ui/login` and sets a PHP session cookie, or returns a different concrete auth clue for the same reproduced journey.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If this path is the first auth-triggering hop you can actually reproduce, treat it as stronger evidence than a guessed Keycloak-first model. On this lab the browser hit `POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false` before the later Keycloak and BusinessFlow login activity. If it never appears in the reproduced journey, keep it inferred only.
recommended_commands.label: Check the legacy suite session-validation hop with cookies

recommended_commands.expected_signal: BusinessFlow health endpoints return HTTP 200 and show that the local BusinessFlow, AFA, and AFF links are still healthy enough to support the login handoff.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use this after the legacy session-validation hop reaches the first named module. On this lab BusinessFlow appeared before the later Keycloak and FireFlow auth checks, so a failing BusinessFlow health response is a real stop point, not just background noise.
recommended_commands.label: Check the BusinessFlow checkpoint through Apache

recommended_commands.command: curl -k -sS -D - 'https://127.0.0.1/FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/?login=1' -o /tmp/asms-fireflow-auth.body && sed -n '1,20p' /tmp/asms-fireflow-auth.body; echo '--- apache ---'; grep -E '/FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/|/FireFlow/XML/CommandsDispatcher|FireFlowBridge.js' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 20
recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Set-Cookie: RT_SID_FireFlow.443=fd55857e44bdd59360ee3c2c12e7be45; path=/; secure; HttpOnly; SameSite=Lax
--- apache ---
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:09 -0400] "GET /FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/?login=1 HTTP/1.1" 200 309
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:10 -0400] "GET /afa/php/JSlib1768164240/FireFlowBridge.js HTTP/1.1" 200 484
recommended_commands.expected_signal: FireFlow auth checks return HTTP 200 and recent Apache lines show CheckAuthentication, CommandsDispatcher, or FireFlowBridge activity in the same customer-visible login minute.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Keep FireFlow inside the same auth handoff for now. On this lab it did not show up as the first named module, but it did appear during sign-in and immediately after `/afa/php/home.php`, which makes it a support-useful checkpoint when the browser reaches home and then stalls.
recommended_commands.label: Check the FireFlow auth-coupled signals

recommended_commands.example_output:
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:00 -0400] "GET /seikan/login/setup HTTP/1.1" 200 217
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "GET /BusinessFlow/login HTTP/1.1" 302 30
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false HTTP/1.1" 200 65
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:02 -0400] "POST //keycloak/realms/master/protocol/openid-connect/token? HTTP/1.1" 200 1562
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:06 -0400] "POST /BusinessFlow/rest/v1/login HTTP/1.1" 200 49
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:09 -0400] "GET /FireFlow/SelfService/CheckAuthentication/?login=1 HTTP/1.1" 200 309
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:10 -0400] "GET /afa/php/JSlib1768164240/FireFlowBridge.js HTTP/1.1" 200 484
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent Apache lines show whether the reproduced login moved from the legacy setup and session-validation paths into BusinessFlow as the first named module, then into Keycloak and FireFlow auth checks, and finally into the PHP home handoff.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use this to keep the auth model honest. On this lab BusinessFlow redirects appeared before submit, Keycloak token paths appeared after the legacy auth-trigger hop, and FireFlow auth checks plus `FireFlowBridge.js` showed up later around the PHP home handoff. If BusinessFlow never lights up for the reproduced minute, keep the failure point earlier. If BusinessFlow lights up but the later Keycloak or FireFlow signals do not, keep the stop point on that later handoff instead of skipping to Metro.
recommended_commands.label: Correlate the authenticated auth handoff in Apache

recommended_commands.example_output:
● keycloak.service - The authentication and authorization server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/keycloak.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:38:32 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 3758 (algosec_keycloa)
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the reproduced journey lights up `/keycloak/` and this service is not running, the auth handoff is failing at Keycloak. On this lab `/keycloak/` appeared after the legacy setup hop and BusinessFlow checkpoint, not as the first post-shell request. If the journey has not reached `/keycloak/` yet, keep Keycloak as a reachable later auth neighbor, not the first proven hop.
recommended_commands.label: Check keycloak.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Ports 8443 and 9000 are listening for Keycloak.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If 8443 or 9000 is missing, focus on Keycloak startup, bind, or health-endpoint problems.
recommended_commands.label: Check Keycloak ports

recommended_commands.expected_signal: The JSON response shows `"status": "UP"`.
healthy_markers: Keycloak database connections async health check
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the readiness endpoint is not UP, Keycloak may be running but not healthy enough to serve login traffic.
recommended_commands.label: Check Keycloak readiness

recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
Server: Apache
Content-Type: application/json;charset=UTF-8
recommended_commands.expected_signal: The local Keycloak OIDC path returns HTTP 200.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the OIDC path fails, Keycloak may be up but not reachable through the expected local path. If it works but your reproduced journey never hits `/keycloak/`, keep Keycloak as reachable and inferred, not observed as the first post-shell hop.
recommended_commands.label: Check Keycloak OIDC path

steps.why_this_matters: The static shell alone does not prove the real auth order. On this lab the first JS-triggered request after the shell was `/seikan/login/setup`, the first session-validation hop was `POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false`, BusinessFlow first appeared as a redirect and later `POST /BusinessFlow/rest/v1/login`, and FireFlow showed up as auth checks during sign-in plus `FireFlowBridge.js` after the PHP home page loaded.

steps.action: Check whether the app branch can do useful work after the authenticated handoff. After login reaches `/afa/php/home.php`, treat the rendered home page itself as the main signal and use the immediate home-shell follow-ups and light Metro refresh traffic as supporting clues before assuming a broader subsystem failure.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here only when the rendered home page itself is not meaningfully usable or when the supporting clues line up with the same stop point. If `/afa/php/home.php` renders the normal home view, treat `FireFlowBridge.js` and the light Metro refresh calls as supporting evidence, not the sole failure gate. If the page renders but Metro heartbeat and post-home refresh traffic are absent or unhealthy, stay on the Metro home-refresh path instead of jumping to broader subsystem guesses.
evidence_to_collect: Auth and app branch evidence from systemctl status, listener output, and local useful-work checks for Keycloak and Metro.
Recent Apache, Keycloak, and Metro clues only after the host and edge checks narrow the likely stop point.
steps.failure_point: ms-metro.service, Metro app traffic, or the Metro JVM
steps.if_fail: Stop here only when the rendered home page itself is not meaningfully usable or when the supporting clues line up with the same stop point. If `/afa/php/home.php` renders the normal home view, treat `FireFlowBridge.js` and the light Metro refresh calls as supporting evidence, not the sole failure gate. If the page renders but Metro heartbeat and post-home refresh traffic are absent or unhealthy, stay on the Metro home-refresh path instead of jumping to broader subsystem guesses.
steps.if_pass: If the app branch looks healthy, move to the stop-point clues.
steps.next_if_pass: If the app branch looks healthy, move to the stop-point clues.

recommended_commands.example_output:
● ms-metro.service - ms-metro Application Container
   Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/ms-metro.service; disabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:39:32 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 6012 (java)
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If Metro is not running, the UI may open only partly or fail after the first page.
recommended_commands.label: Check ms-metro.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Port 8080 is listening for ms-metro.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If port 8080 is missing, the UI backend route has no working target.
recommended_commands.label: Check Metro listener

recommended_commands.command: printf '=== apache ===\n'; grep -E '/afa/php/home.php|FireFlowBridge.js|/afa/php/logo.php|/afa/api/v1/license|/ms-watchdog/v1/api/issues-center/issues/countAll' /var/log/httpd/ssl_access_log | tail -n 40; printf '=== metro ===\n'; grep -E '/afa/getStatus|/afa/api/v1/bridge/refresh|/afa/api/v1/license' /data/ms-metro/logs/localhost_access_log.txt | tail -n 20
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent lines show `/afa/php/home.php`, immediate home-shell follow-ups such as `FireFlowBridge.js` and `logo.php`, and light Metro refresh traffic such as `GET /afa/getStatus`, `POST /afa/api/v1/bridge/refresh`, or `GET /afa/api/v1/license`.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use this to keep the post-home model honest. On this lab the first post-home activity was mostly home-shell wiring plus light Metro refresh traffic. Keep these routes inside the ASMS playbook as supporting clues, not as first-class gates by themselves. In a bounded browser-side check, blocking `FireFlowBridge.js` did not prevent the initial home page from rendering with the normal menu and summary view. If `ms-watchdog` issue-count calls appear from another client address, treat them as a later subsystem candidate unless the reproduced browser minute proves they are your first stop point.
recommended_commands.label: Check supporting post-home follow-up traffic

recommended_commands.expected_signal: The JSON response shows `"isAlive" : true`.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the heartbeat hangs, errors, or returns a different value, Metro is not healthy enough for the ASMS UI path.
recommended_commands.label: Check Metro heartbeat

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent access lines show normal 200 responses for the Metro heartbeat and the light authenticated `/afa/api/v1/...` paths that appear immediately after the home page loads.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If heartbeat works but the post-home `/afa/api/v1/...` lines stop, shift to 4xx or 5xx, or never appear after `/afa/php/home.php`, Metro may be up without serving real home-refresh work. Keep these as stronger clues than `FireFlowBridge.js`, but still do not assume that every later subsystem route has to appear in the first post-home second.
recommended_commands.label: Check Metro app traffic

recommended_commands.expected_signal: The Metro JVM is present, has a stable elapsed runtime, and its CPU, memory, and thread count look reasonable for the current case.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If CPU, memory, or thread count looks unexpectedly high or unstable, treat Metro resource pressure or a stuck JVM as part of the failure.
recommended_commands.label: Check what Metro is busy doing

steps.why_this_matters: The app branch can fail even when Apache/HTTPD and the auth chain look healthy. On this lab the first post-home activity was mostly home-shell wiring plus light Metro refresh traffic, not an immediate fan-out into every subsystem. The earliest observed follow-ups after `/afa/php/home.php` were `FireFlowBridge.js`, `logo.php`, `GET /afa/getStatus`, `POST /afa/api/v1/bridge/refresh`, and `GET /afa/api/v1/license`. In a bounded client-side check, blocking `FireFlowBridge.js` did not stop the home page from rendering with normal menu and summary text, so these post-home routes are better treated as supporting clues than as first-class gates for initial home-page availability.

steps.action: If useful work still stops here, use Apache/HTTPD, Keycloak, and Metro clues to find the first clear stop point.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat the first clear error source as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Auth and app branch evidence from systemctl status, listener output, and local useful-work checks for Keycloak and Metro.
Recent Apache, Keycloak, and Metro clues only after the host and edge checks narrow the likely stop point.
steps.failure_point: Recent service errors in Apache/HTTPD, Keycloak, or Metro
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat the first clear error source as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If the stop point is still unclear, move to a narrower service workflow or escalation path.
steps.next_if_pass: If the stop point is still unclear, move to a narrower service workflow or escalation path.

recommended_commands.command:
rm -f /tmp/asms-journey.cookies /tmp/asms-journey.headers /tmp/asms-journey.body; curl -k -sS -L -c /tmp/asms-journey.cookies -b /tmp/asms-journey.cookies -A 'Mozilla/5.0 (X11; Linux x86_64) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/123.0.0.0 Safari/537.36' -H 'Accept: text/html,application/xhtml+xml,application/xml;q=0.9,*/*;q=0.8' -H 'Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9' -H 'Upgrade-Insecure-Requests: 1' -D /tmp/asms-journey.headers -o /tmp/asms-journey.body https://127.0.0.1/algosec/suite/login; printf '=== headers ===
'; sed -n '1,30p' /tmp/asms-journey.headers; printf '=== cookies ===
'; cat /tmp/asms-journey.cookies 2>/dev/null; printf '=== html markers ===
'; grep -E 'base href=|runtime.js|main.js|styles.css' /tmp/asms-journey.body | head -n 10
recommended_commands.example_output:
HTTP/1.1 302 Found
Location: https://127.0.0.1/algosec-ui/login
<base href="/algosec-ui/">
<script src="runtime.js?v=1768164271" type="module"></script>
<script src="main.js?v=1768164271" type="module"></script>
recommended_commands.expected_signal: The replay proves the legacy suite redirect, the static shell markers, and whether the browser-like startup already picked up cookies before any deeper auth path is tested.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use this as the bounded reproduction anchor for one minute of Apache logs. If it only proves the redirect and static shell, the first real downstream auth or app hop is still unproven.
recommended_commands.label: Replay one browser-like login journey with cookies before chasing neighbors

recommended_commands.example_output:
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "GET /algosec-ui/login HTTP/1.1" 200 13345
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:00 -0400] "GET /seikan/login/setup HTTP/1.1" 200 217
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "GET /BusinessFlow/login HTTP/1.1" 302 30
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:01 -0400] "POST /afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php?clean=false HTTP/1.1" 200 65
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:02 -0400] "POST //keycloak/realms/master/protocol/openid-connect/token? HTTP/1.1" 200 1562
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:06 -0400] "POST /BusinessFlow/rest/v1/login HTTP/1.1" 200 49
127.0.0.1 - - [25/Mar/2026:16:09:09 -0400] "GET /afa/php/home.php HTTP/1.1" 200 35713
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent Apache access lines show the same reproduced journey and make it clear whether the request stopped at the shell, moved through the legacy setup and session-validation paths, reached BusinessFlow as the first named module, and only then handed off through Keycloak, FireFlow, the PHP home page, and Metro-backed app paths.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the reproduced journey only shows /algosec/suite/login and /algosec-ui/ lines, the first real downstream hop is still unproven. If `/seikan/login/setup` appears first, treat it as the first observed JS-triggered post-shell request. If `/afa/php/SuiteLoginSessionValidation.php` is the first auth-triggering line, keep the failure point there unless later BusinessFlow, Keycloak, or FireFlow lines appear. If `/BusinessFlow` lights up but `/afa/api/v1` only appears after `/afa/php/home.php`, treat Metro as a later post-login app branch, not the first auth hop.
recommended_commands.label: Correlate the reproduced minute in Apache access logs

recommended_commands.expected_signal: No obvious disk, permission, startup, or crash errors appear in recent lines.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If recent Apache/HTTPD lines show errors, stay on that clue first.
recommended_commands.label: Review recent Apache/HTTPD logs

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear auth, database, TLS, or startup clue.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If recent Keycloak lines show errors, stay on that clue first.
recommended_commands.label: Review recent Keycloak logs

recommended_commands.expected_signal: No fresh Metro error signatures appear, or the returned lines clearly point to the real Java, dependency, or application failure.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use this only after the traffic and JVM checks if you still need a narrower Java clue.
recommended_commands.label: Review Metro error clues

steps.why_this_matters: Only move to logs after the host, edge, auth branch, and app branch checks. At this point the goal is to find where useful work stops, not to read every log from the bottom.

check_sequence: Check whether the host is under storage, inode, memory, or CPU pressure before blaming the ASMS UI path.
Confirm Apache/HTTPD can answer local UI traffic and still route toward the auth and app branches.
If the login journey gets past the legacy setup hop, confirm BusinessFlow can still answer its local health and login handoff paths before treating the failure as Keycloak-only.
Confirm Keycloak can still do useful auth work, not only that keycloak.service is active.
Confirm ms-metro can still do useful app work, not only that port 8080 is open.
Use FireFlow auth checks as later customer-visible signals in the same journey, especially when the browser reaches `/afa/php/home.php` and then stalls.
Use recent Apache, Keycloak, and Metro clues only after the host and edge checks narrow the first stop point.
evidence_to_collect_next: Host pressure signals from df -h, df -ih, free -h, uptime, journalctl -k, and top CPU output.
Apache edge evidence from systemctl status, ss -lntp, local curl -I, and proxy route snippets under /etc/httpd/conf.d.
Auth and app branch evidence from systemctl status, listener output, and local useful-work checks for Keycloak and Metro.
Recent Apache, Keycloak, and Metro clues only after the host and edge checks narrow the likely stop point.

first_response_steps: Confirm the customer appliance hostname, OS family, and any visible build markers against the lab baseline.

Use the recorded config and log checkpoints for the top services before inferring broader platform issues.

support_focus: Start with AlgoSec product services and direct dependencies before generic Rocky platform services.
Use service state, local port bindings, and proxy-config mismatches before deeper inference when guiding a remote support session.
